did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and machine learning for skin lesion classification. Specifically, it utilizes pre-trained Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models (VGG16, EfficientNet B0, and ResNet50) for feature extraction from the HAM10000 and BCN20000 datasets. It proposes optimized weighted deep feature fusion strategies (AWFS, MOWFS, FOWFS) using a meta-heuristic artificial jellyfish (AJS) algorithm to optimize weights. The final classification is performed using machine learning classifiers including Decision Tree (DT), Naïve Bayesian (NB), Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), and Support Vector Machine (SVM).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of three new optimized weighted deep feature fusion strategies (AWFS, MOWFS, FOWFS) for skin lesion classification. It details specific methodologies including the use of pre-trained CNNs (VGG16, EfficientNet B0, ResNet50), a meta-heuristic artificial jellyfish (AJS) algorithm, and classifiers (DT, NB, MLP, SVM) on the HAM10000 and BCN20000 datasets. The study reports empirical results, accuracy metrics, and statistical validation (Friedman test), which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: Integrated Design of Optimized Weighted Deep Feature Fusion Strategies for Skin Lesion Image Classification
brief_summary: This study addresses skin lesion classification by proposing three optimized weighted deep feature fusion strategies: Adaptive Weighted Feature Set (AWFS), Model-based Optimized Weighted Feature Set (MOWFS), and Feature-based Optimized Weighted Feature Set (FOWFS). The researchers utilized pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG16, EfficientNet B0, and ResNet50) for feature extraction from the HAM10000 and BCN20000 datasets. To optimize the fusion weights, a meta-heuristic artificial jellyfish (AJS) algorithm was employed to adaptively select important features. The performance of these strategies was evaluated using Decision Tree, Naïve Bayesian, Multi-Layer Perceptron, and Support Vector Machine classifiers, measuring accuracy, precision, sensitivity, and F1-score. The results indicated that FOWFS-AJS achieved the best accuracy performance with 94.05% on the HAM10000 dataset and 94.90% on the BCN20000 dataset when using the SVM classifier. Statistical validation via the Friedman test confirmed that FOWFS-AJS performed very well due to its quick converging nature.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis-like lesions, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on 'skin lesion image classification' using pre-trained CNNs for feature extraction and machine learning classifiers (Decision Tree, Naïve Bayesian, Multi-Layer Perceptron, Support Vector Machine) to predict discrete diagnostic classes from the HAM10000 (7 classes) and BCN20000 (8 classes) datasets. Performance is measured via accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The abbreviated form of Human Against Machine dataset, containing 10,000 training images for the detection of pigmented skin lesions. It is a public dataset.
classes_used: Actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease (AKIEC)

use.training: Used for feature extraction using pre-trained CNNs (VGG16, EfficientNet B0, ResNet50) and subsequent classification evaluation.
use.validation: Used for validation accuracy comparison of fused feature sets.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score, AUC-ROC) of the proposed feature fusion strategies.

datasets_used.name: BCN20000
datasets_used.description: A dataset composed of 19,424 dermoscopic images of skin lesions collected from a hospital clinic in Barcelona during the period 2010 to 2016.

use.training: Used for feature extraction using pre-trained CNNs (VGG16, EfficientNet B0, ResNet50) and subsequent classification evaluation.
use.validation: Used for validation accuracy comparison of fused feature sets.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score, AUC-ROC) of the proposed feature fusion strategies.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze experimental results and confirm that FOWFS-AJS performed very well due to its quick converging nature, providing statistical validation of the proposed feature selection strategies.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistical difference between the models or strategies experimented and compared (i.e., the average rankings of the models are equal).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistical difference between the models or strategies experimented and compared (i.e., at least one model's average ranking differs from the others).
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The null hypothesis was rejected because the calculated statistical value (FM = 19.7988) was greater than the critical value (2.9782) at the significance level of 0.05. This indicates that there is a significant difference among the compared models, and the proposed FOWFS-AJS strategy achieved satisfactory performance.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.main_model: Three pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) used for feature extraction: VGG16, EfficientNet B0, and ResNet50. These are combined using optimized weighted deep feature fusion strategies (AWFS, MOWFS, FOWFS) optimized by an Artificial Jellyfish (AJS) algorithm. Final classification is performed using Decision Tree (DT), Naïve Bayesian (NB), Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on large-scale image classification datasets (specifically ImageNet is implied for EfficientNet and standard for VGG/ResNet, though not explicitly named as the source in the text, they are described as 'pre-trained models... previously trained on a large dataset').

VGG16.description: Consists of blocks composed of 2D convolution and max pooling layers. Uses small 3x3 filters. Extracts 512 features.

EfficientNet_B0.description: Developed using multi-objective neural architecture search optimizing accuracy and floating point operations. Uses multiple convolution layers with 3x3 receptive field and mobile inverted bottleneck convolution layer. Extracts 1024 features.

ResNet50.description: Deep network with 5 stages containing 3 convolutional layers and 1 identity block. Uses skip connections to overcome vanishing gradient problem. Extracts 1024 features.

fusion_strategies.AWFS: Adaptive Weighted Feature Set with 3 total weights.
fusion_strategies.MOWFS: Model-based Optimized Weighted Feature Set with 3 total weights.
fusion_strategies.FOWFS: Feature-based Optimized Weighted Feature Set with 3 total weights (dimensions: 1x504, 1x1024, 1x1024).

training_details.method: Transfer learning (feature extraction only). The pre-trained CNNs were not retrained on the skin lesion datasets; their weights were frozen to extract features. The fusion weights were optimized using AJS/GA/PSO. The final classifiers (DT, NB, MLP, SVM) were trained on the fused features.
training_details.loss_function: Mean Squared Error (MSE) of the opted classification model was used as the cost function for the AJS optimization of fusion weights.
training_details.hardware: Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-7200U CPU @ 2.50G Hz with 2.71 GHz processor, 4.00 GB RAM, 64-bit operating system, executed on Google Colab.

classification_or_output_head.best_performing: SVM classifier combined with FOWFS-AJS strategy.

approximate_parameters.VGG16: Not explicitly reported (standard VGG16 has ~138M parameters, but not stated in text)

preprocessing_techniques: The paper states that the study focuses on pre-processing the HAM10000 and BCN20000 skin lesion datasets to select important features, but does not report specific preprocessing techniques (such as resizing, normalization, or artifact removal) applied to the images prior to feature extraction.

The paper mentions that CNN pre-trained architectures can effectively identify and remove artifacts such as noise, but this is cited as a general capability of DL methods rather than a specific step performed in this study's methodology.

No specific preprocessing techniques like resizing, cropping, padding, normalization, color correction, hair/artifact removal, segmentation masks, denoising, filtering, registration, patch extraction, or quality control are explicitly reported as being applied to the input images in this work.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper does not report the use of data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, or synthetic data generation) during training or dataset construction. The study utilized pre-trained CNN models (VGG16, EfficientNet B0, ResNet50) for feature extraction via transfer learning, where the pre-trained weights were frozen, and focused on feature fusion strategies and meta-heuristic optimization (AJS, GA, PSO) for weight selection rather than image-level augmentation.

conclusions: The proposed Feature-based Optimized Weighted Feature Set with Artificial Jellyfish (FOWFS-AJS) strategy achieved the best accuracy performance among the tested methods, reaching 94.05% on the HAM10000 dataset and 94.90% on the BCN20000 dataset when combined with the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier.

FOWFS-AJS outperformed other proposed strategies (AWFS, MOWFS-AJS) and baseline methods (Combined Feature Set, GA, PSO) in terms of validation accuracy, precision, sensitivity, F1-score, and AUC-ROC across both datasets.
The FOWFS-AJS strategy demonstrated superior computational efficiency, showing better performance regarding both feature acquisition time and classification time compared to other strategies.

Statistical validation using the Friedman test (at significance level alpha = 0.05) confirmed that there is a significant difference among the compared models, rejecting the null hypothesis and supporting the satisfactory performance of the FOWFS-AJS strategy.

The use of the meta-heuristic Artificial Jellyfish (AJS) algorithm for optimizing fusion weights provided a good starting point for initialization, helping to mitigate the chances of exploding or vanishing gradients.

The study demonstrates that adaptively choosing weights of pre-trained networks (VGG16, EfficientNet B0, ResNet50) using optimized feature fusion strategies effectively selects important features for skin cancer classification, addressing challenges related to cross-domain adaptability and robustness.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on two public datasets (HAM10000 and BCN20000) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
No data augmentation techniques were employed; the model performance may be sensitive to the specific distribution and potential biases inherent in the HAM10000 and BCN20000 datasets.

The CNN feature extractors (VGG16, EfficientNet B0, ResNet50) were used in a frozen transfer learning mode (weights not fine-tuned), which may limit the model's ability to adapt to domain-specific features of skin lesions compared to fine-tuned approaches.

The paper does not report specific image preprocessing steps (e.g., resizing, normalization, artifact removal), relying on the implicit capabilities of pre-trained CNNs, which may obscure potential data leakage or preprocessing biases.

Computational experiments were conducted on a standard consumer-grade CPU (Intel Core i5) via Google Colab, which may not reflect performance on specialized hardware (e.g., GPUs) typically used in clinical deployment.
The study focuses on feature-level fusion rather than decision-level fusion, which may not fully capture the complementary strengths of different architectures as effectively as ensemble methods.

The authors acknowledge that the work is limited to three pre-trained networks and suggests future work on other datasets and meta-heuristic optimizers, indicating the current findings are preliminary.
No conflict of interest was declared, and the research received no external funding, suggesting potential lack of industry bias but also limited resources for extensive validation.
